Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd spend a lot of hours studying each week, but it wasn't really organized in any structured way. I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, or study in bursts when I felt like it. Sometimes I'd have a productive day and then not touch anything for a couple of days. I know I should have been more consistent, but I just went with whatever felt right in the moment.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd sometimes go days without doing much, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and put in a ton of hours. My extracurriculars took up a lot of my mental energy, so studying happened whenever I could squeeze it in. Regular schedules just never really worked for me, even though I know they probably should.
Q3: Usually it was either because I remembered an assignment was due or because I got a notification about something. Sometimes my study group would mention something in our chat and I'd log in to check what they were talking about. I also logged in when I wanted to watch videos or catch up on lectures I'd missed, which happened pretty often given my attendance.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a real routine for me. There were weeks where I logged in almost daily, but that was more about catching up than habit. The peer support group I was part of helped a bit because they'd remind me to check things, so that created some consistency. But left to my own devices, I'd probably forget to log in for days.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often play them while commuting or doing chores, which made it feel less like a burden. The peer interactions were also helpful because hearing other students explain things sometimes clicked better than the formal materials. I watched a lot of video content, probably more than most people.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums. Posting there felt awkward and performative, like I was just doing it to be seen rather than actually learning. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary readings because they felt overwhelming and I could usually get by without them. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it was easy to let it slide.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to something I needed to submit or would be tested on. I also valued things that fit into my life easily, like audio content I could listen to anywhere. If something required me to sit down and focus for hours with no clear payoff, I'd usually find reasons to skip it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like extras I could ignore when things got busy. Required ones had this weight to them because I knew they'd affect my grade. I'd tell myself I'd get to the optional stuff eventually, but honestly, I rarely did. There's only so much energy to go around, and required work always won.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute. I also used notes from my peer group and sometimes looked things up on YouTube when the course materials didn't make sense. My actual learning was spread across a bunch of different places.
Q10: There were definitely times when I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three times while walking around, or when my study group would meet up in person and work through problems together. I'd also spend time just thinking through concepts without having anything to click on or submit.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download videos or PDFs and then work with them offline for days. It was easier than constantly logging in, especially when my internet was spotty or I was on the go. The LMS would just show that one download, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My attendance dropped because I was juggling extracurriculars and just felt stretched thin. Even though I wasn't super anxious about it, I'd sometimes just feel disconnected from the course and struggle to care. Those were the weeks where I'd barely log in.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or had a lot going on with my activities outside class, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. I'd still be studying in my own way, but the platform itself felt like extra effort. Confusion also played a role, like if I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes avoid it rather than ask for help.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere. Sometimes I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything. Being logged in doesn't mean being present, and I had a lot of those zombie study sessions.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on things I found boring or confusing. Even when I knew an assignment was important, if it felt tedious, I'd push it off until the last minute. It wasn't that I didn't care about my grades, I just had trouble making myself do things that didn't feel immediately rewarding. I'm pretty resilient when crunch time comes though.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly become super active on the LMS, downloading everything and working intensively. Without deadlines, I'd drift and lose focus. They created this urgency that I apparently needed to actually get things done.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback made me feel like I was on the right track and sometimes motivated me to engage more. But if feedback was vague or came too late, it felt pointless. I think I would have engaged more consistently if feedback had been quicker and more specific.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really push me to stay consistent. Since I could access most things whenever, I'd let stuff pile up. The peer support intervention actually helped because it gave me people to check in with. Without that social element, I probably would have drifted even more.
Q19: Having video content was great for my learning style, so that made engagement easier. But the discussion forums felt forced and didn't match how I actually learn. I also think having so many online courses made it hard to keep track of everything. Simpler navigation and clearer priorities would have helped me stay focused.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but keeps getting stuck. They might be clicking around, watching videos, reading materials, but never feeling confident enough to actually submit work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to follow through. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me. My LMS activity wasn't always consistent, but I still did okay because I studied a lot offline and had my peer group to learn with. Some people just don't need to be constantly logged in to understand the material. The LMS is one tool, not the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and what you submitted. That's useful for tracking basic participation. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether you actually understood something, how much you studied offline, and what your mental state was. They're like seeing someone's footprints but not knowing if they were running or just wandering.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. It's about being mentally present and curious. I wasn't always engaged in that way, even when my activity looked decent on paper.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on my peer group for learning, or how many hours I spent listening to lectures while doing other things. It also wouldn't capture the times I was happy and motivated versus just going through the motions. My engagement was real, but it happened in ways that don't leave digital footprints.
